content.problemStatement:
Each lemonade costs $5. Customers arrive one by one and pay using a $5, $10, or $20 bill.
Initially, the shop has no money for change.

For each customer:


If they pay with $5 → no change is needed.
If they pay with $10 → you must give back $5.
If they pay with $20 → you must give back $15 using available $5 and/or $10 bills.
The function must determine whether it is possible to serve all customers in order while always giving correct change.

Function DeclarationFunction NamecanProvideChangecanProvideChangecanProvideChange – This function checks whether it is possible to give correct change to every customer at a lemonade stand.

Parameters
customerBillscustomerBillscustomerBills : A reference to an array of integers where each element represents the bill paid by a customer.

Each value is one of {5, 10, 20}.
Each lemonade costs exactly $5.


numberOfBillsnumberOfBillsnumberOfBills : The number of elements in the customerBills array.
Return Value
Returns truetruetrue if correct change can be provided to every customer.
Returns falsefalsefalse otherwise.
Constraints
1≤T≤1051 \leq T \leq 10^51≤T≤105
1≤n≤1051 \leq n \leq 10^51≤n≤105
customerBills[i]∈{5,10,20}customerBills[i] \in \{5, 10, 20\}customerBills[i]∈{5,10,20}
Sum of nnn over all test cases does not exceed 10510^5105

Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases.
For each test case:

The first line contains a single integer nnn — the number of customers.
The second line contains nnn space-separated integers representing the array customerBillscustomerBillscustomerBills.




Output Format

For each test case, print a boolean value:

truetruetrue if correct change can be provided to all customers.
falsefalsefalse otherwise.
constraints: Sum of n over all test cases does not exceed 100000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer n – the number of customers. The second line contains n space‑separated integers, each being 5, 10 or 20, representing the bill each customer pays.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "true" if the shop can give correct change to every customer, otherwise output "false".

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test case fails at the last customer because only one $5 is left. Second fails at the last $20 for the same reason. Third succeeds as change can always be given.
In the first case the shop can give $15 as one $10 and one $5. In the second case the first customer pays with $10 but the shop has no $5 to return.
After processing the first seven customers the shop has 1 five and 2 tens. The eighth $20 can be given change (10+5) leaving 0 fives and 1 ten. The last $20 cannot be given change.

solution.approach:
We simulate the process keeping only the count of $5 and $10 bills the shop currently has. For each bill:
- If it is 5, increment fiveCount.
- If it is 10, we need one $5 as change; if none exists the answer is false, otherwise decrement fiveCount and increment tenCount.
- If it is 20, we prefer to give one $10 and one $5 (preserves $5 bills). If that is not possible, we try to give three $5 bills. If neither is possible the answer is false.
If we finish all customers without failing, the answer is true.
The algorithm is O(n) per test case and uses O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(total number of customers) i.e., O(Σ n) across all test cases.
